name: Electrolux Trilobite
short_description: The world's first commercially available autonomous robot vacuum, using ultrasonic sensors to map rooms and dock itself for recharging.
long_description: Launched in 2001 by Swedish appliance giant Electrolux, the Trilobite was the first mass-market robotic vacuum cleaner — predating the iRobot Roomba by a year. A prototype debuted on BBC's Tomorrow's World in 1997, demonstrating ultrasonic-sensor-based obstacle avoidance and semi-structured room traversal. The commercial ZA1 model retailed for around $1,500 and could run for up to an hour before automatically returning to its charging dock. A 2004 revision (ZA2) added infrared stair-detection sensors. The Trilobite proved that autonomous room-mapping and self-docking were viable in a consumer product, establishing the design template every robot vacuum has followed since.
ai_note: Rule-based ultrasonic navigation and semi-structured room-mapping — no learning or ML, but autonomous sensing and docking.

images.caption: Electrolux Trilobite robot vacuum photographed in 2006.
images.alt_text: A circular, silver-grey Electrolux Trilobite robotic vacuum cleaner sitting on a hard floor, viewed from a slight angle above showing its disc-shaped body and control panel.

interactions.title: Set it to clean while you're at work
interactions.description: On the ZA2 you program a weekly schedule — for instance, run at 10am every day while you're out — and come home to floors already done, VCR-style timer programming and all.

interactions.title: Pick a mode for the mess
interactions.description: Choose Normal (traces the walls first to size up the room, then fills in the open floor), Quick (skips the wall pass), or Spot (works a roughly one-square-metre patch twice for a spill).

interactions.title: Fence it off with a magnetic strip
interactions.description: Lay a magnetic strip across a doorway — even tuck it under a rug up to 15mm thick — to mark a no-go boundary the Trilobite won't cross, with no wiring or app required.

interactions.title: Let it find its own way home
interactions.description: When the cleaning cycle finishes or the battery runs low after about an hour, the Trilobite navigates back to its charging base on its own and docks to recharge.

interactions.title: Watch it listen its way around the furniture
interactions.description: The robot sweeps the room steered by ultrasound — a gold-plated 60kHz sonar with eight microphones catching the echoes off walls and chair legs — letting it slip within an inch of an object without bumping it.

interactions.title: Trust it at the top of the stairs
interactions.description: On the ZA2 an infrared sensor watches for drop-offs, so when the robot reaches a stair edge or ledge it changes direction instead of tumbling over.

interactions.title: Marvel at it on the telly
interactions.description: Long before owning one, audiences met the disc-shaped cleaner as a prototype demonstrated on the BBC's Tomorrow's World, where its self-driving ultrasonic navigation was shown off to a national audience.

interactions.title: Read the room's time budget off the display
interactions.description: Because Normal mode traces the perimeter first to estimate how long the job will take, you could glance at the panel to see the remaining run time before recharge — a tiny progress contract between machine and owner.

interactions.title: A roaming carpet for the cat
interactions.description: A low, slow, predictable disc that patrols the floor on a timer is exactly the kind of object a pet learns to ride or stalk — the same emergent play later robot vacuums became famous for.
